Title: Selective fertilization with phosphite allows unhindered growth of cotton plants expressing the ptxD gene while suppressing weeds
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phosphite oxidation / phosphorus assimilation enabling pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes phosphite (Phi) into orthophosphate (Pi), enabling metabolizable phosphorus production in planta
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream nutrient-conversion step supplying usable phosphate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the metabolizable phosphorus source used for growth
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential phosphorus nutrient supporting plant growth and biomass accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Confers the ability to utilize phosphite as the sole phosphorus source, promoting cotton growth and indirectly suppressing weeds under phosphite fertilization
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enabling step for phosphite utilization; not established as native rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in transgenic cotton lines by qRT-PCR; constitutively expressed in tested transformants
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphite versus phosphate fertilization; low-phosphorus artificial substrate and natural field soils; competition with weeds including glyphosate-resistant Amaranthus palmeri
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in cotton; qRT-PCR; PTXD enzyme activity assay; Southern blot; growth assays under phosphite as sole P source; weed competition experiments in artificial substrate and natural soils; biomass and height measurements
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ptxD-expressing cotton plants grew normally with phosphite as the sole phosphorus source, whereas WT and null segregants were strongly growth inhibited. 2. ptxD transgenic cotton outcompeted broadleaf and grass weeds under phosphite fertilization in inert substrate and multiple natural soils. 3. The system effectively suppressed glyphosate-resistant Palmer amaranth while improving transgenic cotton biomass and height under phosphite treatment.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful transgene for engineering phosphite-based selective fertilization and nonherbicidal weed management in cotton and potentially other crops; can be stacked with crop improvement traits; future deployment may benefit from root-specific promoters to reduce constitutive expression concerns
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires transgenic deployment and phosphite-based fertilization regime; long-term effects on soil microflora under field conditions remain to be investigated
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper’s Results focus on nutrient use and weed suppression enabled by ptxD rather than endogenous phosphorus metabolism genes. PTXD activity correlated with transcript abundance in selected lines. The effect is mediated by converting absorbed phosphite into phosphate, allowing crop advantage under low-P conditions.
